comments.body: So something changed after some system updates? Do you have FastQC logs from before the updates to compare with? Can you share the relevant Nextflow code and configuration to show how you are running fastqc? Do the task logs (.command.err, .command.out, .command.log) reveal any errors or log anything java related? Some node/cluster specific configuration might be being 'picked up' at runtime. Are you running fastqc in a Conda environment?

@Steve - Question updated. There were no system/software updates, only resource requests for other processes. This allowed more tasks to run at the same time, which hasn't impacted the runtime of other tasks, just FastQC. It is run inside a conda env, and java is installed within that env as well.

So the 'updates' are just changes you made to your resource requests in your nextflow.config. Try moving conda.enabled = true to outside of your process selector. I would put this line at the top of your nextflow.config. On the following line set conda.cacheDir = /some/path'. Then, you can replace process.conda = "$conda_envs/my_env" with conda = '0.12.1'. Use the version you want. Use cpus = 1 and memory can simply be: memory = { 1.GB * task.attempt }. I would also simplify the command to something like fastqc -q ${reads}. Avoid -o here, use the publishDir directive instead.

It might be worth including a minimal working example with just the FastQC process and workflow blocks, as well as the configuration you are using. When you run your workflow, approximately how many FastQC jobs are submitted? I guess my question is, what does it take to start seeing the slowdown?

I don't think conda.enabeled matters within the process selector or within the environment profile. You're right, it is more appropriate within the environment profile, so it applies to all processes you run together. The slurm queue must remain within the process selector - it's not respected within the environment profile (no idea why, easy workaround). cpus = 1 is fine, or fine as 2. FastQC is single-threaded per file, so you're right I'm over-allocating. You're memory statement is a different scaling than I want. Valid only when maxRetries < 3.
publishDir only works when files remain within the Nextflow directory - we're running from different places, so we have to do (or have chosen to do) our own file handling.

We usually run batches of 12 samples - 12 FastQC processes with 12 TrimGalore processes, which finishes and beings STAR processes, which finish and begin de-duplication processes, etc etc. We don't see this issue with 2-4 samples (my testing handful). I've never seen this running a single instance of FastQC. So it's definitely an issue of scale, I just can't figure out where. Other processes (TrimGalore, STAR, etc) don't demonstrate this behavior

Try setting process.executor = 'slurm' instead. Then you could set process.queue = 'LocalQ' and override it in the process selectors as necessary. So I wonder if this has something to do with how/where the results are being written to. Are you reading from /my/drive and writing to it? Is /my/drive fast or slow disk? Even if you run from multiple locations, you should still write the output files to the process working directory. Writing to directories outside of this will break -resume and portability etc. Use publishDir with mode: 'copy'. You can even parameterise thepath.

Doing this would let you run the workflow somewhere using fast disk (e.g. TMPDIR), then when each task finishes, it would publish the files to /my/drive. The difference would be that instead of having multiple processes (from multiple nodes, potentially) writing to /my/drive, only the nextflow process would have this responsibility. File publishing is asynchronous.
Sorry for the delayed reply - your process.XXX suggestion was spot on, thank you. Silly mistake on my part.

We are reading from a network drive (but not saturating the disk I/O or bandwidth, we've had IT monitoring). We're using a TMPDIR for intermediate files (local drive, NVMe protocol). Then we put finished analyses on the network drive. You're right, we're handling it with multiple processes currently. This has been pushed lower on my todo, so I'll test and return when I can.
Just to close the circle from my end, I've been reassigned and won't be looking into this further.

title: FastQC Stalling Under Heavy System Load (JRE/JVM Issue)
body:
Terrible title, and I'll update if a more effective way of asking can be suggested.

Problem

We're running a bioinformatics pipeline that uses FastQC for quality control. The pipeline is written in Nextflow and uses Slurm for job management. Originally, FastQC would take 5-30min to run on a set of samples. However, after updates allowed for significantly greater job parallelization, FastQC will now take up to 15hrs to finish.

The jobs don't die, they don't fail, they just kinda hang out not really running. This does seem to occur during file-reading operations (based on the log files from FastQC).

We're pretty sure this is a JVM/JRE issue, we're just not sure how/why (or even how to google it well).

Repeatability

This doesn't seem to depend on which samples are used.
It's very repeatable - reduce the Slurm allocation and runtimes come back down, increase the allocations and runtimes go up. We've demonstrated the impact on the same and different sets of samples.
As far as we can tell, it doesn't impact any other processes (trimGalore, several Sentieon tools, RNAseQC, RSeQC).

Checks

Nextflow logs indicate the we're allocating plenty of resources to each task - currently using ~40% of the allocated RAM, and it will never go over 50% CPU usage (FastQC is single-threaded, we allocated 2 just to be sure).

We've checked with our cluster IT - the system is not under excessively heavy compute load, the network is below 50% utilization, and disks I/O is below 50% utilization.

We've attempted to increase the ulimit for the server and for the user - no impact.

Github Issue

Setting

Nextflow 24.04.2.5914
Slurm
Ubuntu 22.04
OpenJDK version "21-internal"
OpenJDK Runtime Environment (build 21-internal-adhoc.conda.src)
OpenJDK 64-Bit Server VM (build 21-internal-adhoc.conda.src, mixed mode, sharing)

Update 1

To clarify, there was no system update.
The change was to resource requests from slurm for other processes, allowing significantly greater process concurrency.

The Nextflow run-directory is clean. There's nothing abnormal in the .command.{begin,err,log,out,etc} (I assume b/c the jobs aren't failing).

The Nextflow output files match ones run before any changes were made, same for log files from fastqc itself.

Nextflow setup:

withName: 'run_fastqc1|run_fastqc2' {
  executor = 'slurm'
  queue = 'LocalQ'
  errorStrategy = 'retry'
  maxRetries = 2
  cpus = 2
  memory = { 1.GB * 2 ** (task.attempt - 1)}
  conda.enabled = true
  process.conda = "$conda_envs/my_env"
}

FastQC command:

fastqc --nogroup -f fastq --threads $cpus -o /my/drive/my/path/ fastq_R1.fastq.gz fastq_R2.fastq.gz
